steps.title: Map the two sentences
steps.content: Sentence 1: the researchers tasted the stew and found it mild and calming. Sentence 2: knowing dishes were sometimes named for their effects, they theorized the name "unwinding" described its function. The second sentence is a conclusion drawn from the first sentence's observation.

steps.title: Identify the needed relationship
steps.content: Observation → conclusion built on that observation calls for a cause-effect transition. "Therefore" signals exactly that: because they experienced the calming effect, they reached this theory about the name.

steps.title: Eliminate the wrong relationships
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Therefore,': marks the second sentence as a conclusion drawn from the first — the relationship the passage actually has. ✓
  • Choice B — 'Alternately,': signals a different option or alternative explanation, but sentence 2 is the only explanation offered — nothing is being swapped in. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Nevertheless,': signals contrast, but sentence 2 doesn't push back against sentence 1 — it builds on it. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Likewise,': signals a parallel similar case, but sentence 2 isn't a second instance of the same thing — it's an inference. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
